title: New York - New Jersey Job Recovery Expected to Continue in 1998

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York research note from April 1998 projects regional employment growth of 1.3 percent (approximately 149,000 new jobs) for the New York-New Jersey region in 1998, with modest deceleration from 1997 levels due to anticipated slowing of U.S. economic growth. The authors also assess that Asian market turmoil would have minor direct negative impact on regional exported goods while a WTO financial services agreement would improve employment prospects.
key_points: Regional employment expected to rise 1.3 percent, adding roughly 149,000 new jobs in 1998
New York State, New York City, and New Jersey each projected to record employment gains, though smaller than 1997
Anticipated slowing of U.S. economy growth expected to modestly brake regional job growth
Direct negative impact on New York-New Jersey exported goods from Asian market turmoil assessed as minor
WTO financial services liberalization agreement expected to improve regional employment prospects
Authors James Orr and Rae D. Rosen are senior economists in the Fed's research and public information areas
why_it_matters: This source is a 28-year-old regional employment forecast with no relevance to current market structure or macro catalysts. It predates modern market structure research by decades and does not address any topics within the scope of market structure (trading mechanisms, market plumbing, order flow, exchange operations) or macro catalysts (central bank policy, rate decisions, economic data releases with market-moving implications).
